seed.title: The Coming Storm
seed.text: We stand at the edge of the greatest employment disruption in human history. Unlike past revolutions that displaced muscle, AI threatens to displace minds — and this time, there may be nowhere left to run.

1-1.title: Speed Kills
1-1.text: The Industrial Revolution took generations to unfold. AI is rewriting entire industries in months. Our social safety nets, designed for gradual change, are tissue paper against this hurricane of disruption.

1-2.title: Who Owns Tomorrow?
1-2.text: As algorithms generate wealth without workers, we face a stark choice: will the profits of automation enrich the few who own the machines, or will we find new ways to share the abundance?

1-3.title: The Last Human Jobs
1-3.text: We comfort ourselves that creativity, care, and human connection are irreplaceable. But what happens when AI writes better novels, diagnoses with more empathy, and never tires of listening?

1-4.title: Identity Crisis
1-4.text: For centuries, we've defined ourselves by our work — 'I am a teacher,' 'I am an engineer.' When the machines do it better, who are we? The psychological reckoning may cut deeper than the economic one.

1-5.title: History's False Comfort
1-5.text: Yes, the Luddites were wrong. New jobs always emerged. But past patterns assumed human adaptability outpaced technological change. This time, the machines learn faster than we do.

1-6.title: The Political Powder Keg
1-6.text: Mass unemployment breeds extremism. When millions lose purpose alongside paychecks, democracies crumble. The tech utopians building our future seem dangerously naive about the past.

2-1.title: Exponential vs Linear
2-1.text: Human institutions evolve linearly while AI improves exponentially. By the time Congress debates retraining programs, the jobs they're training for will already be obsolete.

2-2.title: The Overnight Collapse
2-2.text: When GPT-4 launched, copywriters discovered their profession evaporating not over years, but weeks. Every knowledge worker should study that canary in the coal mine.

2-3.title: Regulatory Paralysis
2-3.text: Lawmakers who struggle with email attempt to regulate neural networks. The gap between technological reality and political comprehension has never been wider — or more dangerous.

2-4.title: The Skill Cliff
2-4.text: Traditional education assumes decades of relevance. But why master radiology when AI reads scans better? The half-life of human expertise shrinks toward zero.

2-5.title: The New Feudalism
2-5.text: A handful of companies control the AI that controls everything else. Without intervention, we're building a world where Bezos and Zuckerberg's successors own the means of cognition.

2-6.title: UBI's False Promise
2-6.text: Universal Basic Income sounds humane until you realize it might become universal basic irrelevance — a pittance to keep the obsolete masses docile while the AI-owning class ascends to godhood.

2-7.title: Data as Labor
2-7.text: Every click, every photo, every conversation trains the AIs that replace us. We're not just the product; we're unwitting collaborators in our own obsolescence.

2-8.title: The Empathy Illusion
2-8.text: An AI therapist never judges, never tires, remembers everything. When machines simulate care better than humans provide it, what happens to the caring professions?

2-9.title: Creative Obsolescence
2-9.text: We said AI could never create, only imitate. Then it started winning art contests and writing better poetry. The last human refuge is crumbling.

2-10.title: Touch Hunger
2-10.text: Perhaps human connection remains irreplaceable. But in a world of deepfakes and perfect AI companions, will we even trust that the human we're touching is real?

2-11.title: The Experience Economy
2-11.text: If AI handles production, humans might shift to pure experience — travel, adventure, relationships. But this assumes wealth to purchase experiences, which requires... jobs.

2-12.title: Protestant Work Collapse
2-12.text: The Protestant work ethic built modern capitalism. Without work as virtue, moral purpose, and social organizing principle, do our societies simply... dissolve?

2-13.title: The Leisure Trap
2-13.text: Keynes predicted 15-hour work weeks and worried about excess leisure. He never imagined leisure forced upon us while a lucky few work 80-hour weeks managing the machines.

2-14.title: Status Without Work
2-14.text: Stripped of job titles, how do we signal status? Instagram followers? Gaming achievements? The new hierarchies might make the old ones look dignified.

2-15.title: The Dignity Deficit
2-15.text: There's deep human dignity in being needed, in contributing. No amount of VR entertainment can fill the void of feeling fundamentally unnecessary.

2-16.title: The Centaur Myth
2-16.text: Human-AI collaboration sounds nice — until you realize the AI does 95% while the human becomes a rubber stamp. Centaurs were mythical for a reason.

2-17.title: Winner-Take-All Acceleration
2-17.text: Technology already concentrates wealth; AI supercharges this. The gap between AI-haves and have-nots makes today's inequality look like a rounding error.

2-18.title: The Retraining Delusion
2-18.text: 'Learn to code' became a cruel joke when AI started coding. Every proposed retraining solution assumes a stable target, but the ground won't stop shifting.

2-19.title: Weimar 2.0
2-19.text: Economic desperation plus technological disruption equals political extremism. We're one mass layoff away from movements that make current populism look tame.

2-20.title: The Blame Game
2-20.text: When jobs vanish, humans need scapegoats. Immigrants, elites, the 'other' — history shows where this leads. AI unemployment could unleash humanity's darkest impulses.

2-21.title: Tech Bro Blindness
2-21.text: Silicon Valley's solution to everything: 'just build an app.' They're optimizing humanity out of the equation while living in bubbles that insulate them from consequences.

2-22.title: Democracy's Death Spiral
2-22.text: Democracy assumes an informed, employed citizenry with skin in the game. Mass unemployment plus AI-generated propaganda equals the perfect storm for authoritarianism.

3-1.title: The 18-Month Rule
3-1.text: Every capability you think AI won't have for a decade, it gains in 18 months. Planning for gradual change is planning for extinction.

3-2.title: Institutional Lag
3-2.text: Universities still teach 4-year degrees for jobs that AI will eliminate before graduation. The mismatch between educational and technological timescales is tragic comedy.

3-3.title: The Boiling Frog
3-3.text: Each AI advance seems incremental — just a better chatbot, just faster image generation. We won't notice we're cooked until we're served for dinner.

3-4.title: Content Farm Collapse
3-4.text: SEO writers, stock photographers, voice actors — entire cottage industries evaporated overnight. They were the test cases; we're all next.

3-5.title: The Quality Paradox
3-5.text: Ironically, AI often produces better work than the humans it replaces. Hard to argue for human jobs when the machine writes clearer, thinks faster, never sleeps.

3-6.title: Fax Machine Governance
3-6.text: Congress still uses fax machines while debating AI regulation. The technological IQ gap between rulers and ruled has never been more dangerous.

3-7.title: Lobbying Supremacy
3-7.text: Tech companies spending billions on lobbying face lawmakers who need staffers to open PDFs. Guess who wins that fight?

3-8.title: International Race
3-8.text: Even if one nation slows AI to protect jobs, others won't. The choice: embrace unemployment or become economically irrelevant.

3-9.title: The Union Collapse
3-9.text: Labor unions assume human workers to organize. What leverage do you have when your entire profession can be downloaded?

3-10.title: Medical School Folly
3-10.text: Eight years training to diagnose worse than an AI that trains in eight seconds. Medical education is preparing students for a world that won't exist.

3-11.title: The Apprenticeship End
3-11.text: Master-apprentice knowledge transfer assumes the master's skills matter. When AI surpasses masters, what's left to teach?

3-12.title: Compute Inequality
3-12.text: In the AI age, computational power equals economic power. The rich don't just have more money; they have more intelligence at their disposal.

3-13.title: API Aristocracy
3-13.text: Access to advanced AI APIs becomes the new class divider. Those with keys to GPT-X rule; everyone else serves or starves.

3-14.title: The Subscription Trap
3-14.text: You'll own nothing and be happy — because the AI you need to survive requires monthly payments you can't afford without the job it took.

3-15.title: Digital Breadcrumbs
3-15.text: UBI might keep bodies alive but souls wither. Imagine billions maintained like pets while their former-peer AIs reshape reality.

3-16.title: The Control Mechanism
3-16.text: UBI seems generous until you realize it's the perfect social control tool. Disagree with the system? Your monthly payment has encountered an error.

3-17.title: Unpaid Training
3-17.text: Every CAPTCHA you solve trains AI to not need you. We're literally teaching our replacements for free.

3-18.title: The Data Dignity Movement
3-18.text: Some propose paying people for their data. But what happens when AI no longer needs new data, just computational power?

3-19.title: Digital Sharecropping
3-19.text: We farm engagement for platforms, generating data-crops we don't own. The digital economy perfected exploitation before AI made it obsolete.

3-20.title: The Feedback Loop
3-20.text: AI trained on human data becomes superhuman, making human data worthless. We bootstrap our replacements then wonder why we're replaced.

3-21.title: Therapeutic Unemployment
3-21.text: When AI therapists never break confidentiality, never judge, and cost pennies, human therapists become a luxury good for those who can afford authenticity.

3-22.title: The Care Uncanny Valley
3-22.text: AI care feels almost right — which makes it deeply wrong. The simulation of empathy might be worse than honest indifference.

3-23.title: Prompt Engineer Priests
3-23.text: For a brief moment, 'prompt engineers' were the new creative class. Then AI learned to prompt itself. Even our collaboration with AI becomes obsolete.

3-24.title: Infinite Content
3-24.text: When AI generates infinite perfect content, human creation becomes either pointless hobby or stubborn resistance. Most will choose Netflix.

3-25.title: The Authenticity Premium
3-25.text: 'Human-made' might become a luxury label, like 'organic.' But how do you prove it when AI can fake any human signature?

3-26.title: Digital Hermits
3-26.text: Trust in human interaction collapses when anyone could be an AI. The loneliness epidemic becomes the loneliness default.

3-27.title: The Skin Hunger Economy
3-27.text: Physical touch might be the last human monopoly. Expect the rise of professional huggers, hand-holders, human contact workers.

3-28.title: Reality Authentication
3-28.text: We'll need blockchain verification for human interactions. 'This conversation certified 100% human' — until AI fakes that too.

3-29.title: Experience Inequality
3-29.text: The employed class travels the world; the unemployed travel VR. Real experience becomes another form of wealth concentration.

3-30.title: The Purpose Vacuum
3-30.text: Remove work from 'work-life balance' and what remains? For many, life without work isn't liberation — it's existential horror.

3-31.title: Neo-Monasticism
3-31.text: Expect revival of religious and spiritual movements as humans desperately seek meaning beyond economic function. The AI age might birth new religions.

3-32.title: Productivity Poison
3-32.text: We're so programmed for productivity that purposelessness feels like death. The hardest adjustment might be psychological, not economic.

3-33.title: The Boredom Epidemic
3-33.text: Endless leisure sounds nice until month six. Expect skyrocketing rates of addiction, depression, and self-destructive behavior among the permanently leisured.

3-34.title: Enforced Creativity
3-34.text: 'Everyone's an artist now!' they'll say, ignoring that forced creativity is just another form of make-work, especially when AI out-creates us all.

3-35.title: The Follower Economy
3-35.text: Social media metrics become the new resume. Your worth measured in likes, shares, engagement — the algorithmic popularity contest from hell.

3-36.title: Competitive Consumption
3-36.text: Without career achievements, consumption becomes competition. Who has the newest gadget, the best experience, the most exclusive access.

3-37.title: The Helping Impulse
3-37.text: Humans need to feel helpful. When machines need no help, we're reduced to helping each other cope with being unneeded — recursive purposelessness.

3-38.title: The 5% Solution
3-38.text: Human-AI teams where humans contribute 5% aren't partnerships — they're fig leaves for our obsolescence. Token human involvement.

3-39.title: Responsibility Without Power
3-39.text: The worst position: legally responsible for AI decisions you don't understand. All liability, no actual control — the human rubber stamp.

3-40.title: The Deskilling Spiral
3-40.text: The more AI helps, the less skilled humans become, justifying more AI help. The partnership devolves into dependency into irrelevance.

3-41.title: Network Effect Tyranny
3-41.text: AI amplifies network effects exponentially. The biggest AI gets the most data, improves fastest, attracts more users — unstoppable monopoly dynamics.

3-42.title: Regional Devastation
3-42.text: Entire regions built on now-automated industries face collapse. The Rust Belt was a preview; the AI Belt will encompass whole nations.

3-43.title: The Moving Target
3-43.text: 'Future-proof' careers are mirages. By the time you retrain for the 'safe' job, AI already conquered that hilltop too.

3-44.title: Digital Brownshirts
3-44.text: Mass unemployment plus social media equals perfect recruitment for extremism. Angry, purposeless millions are history's most dangerous ingredient.

3-45.title: The Scapegoat Algorithm
3-45.text: When jobs disappear, who gets blamed? The immigrants? The elites? The machines? History suggests we'll choose the most vulnerable targets.

3-46.title: Neo-Luddism Rising
3-46.text: Expect violent anti-AI movements. When smashing machines seems like the only agency left, some will light the torches.

3-47.title: The Purity Spiral
3-47.text: Unemployment breeds extremism breeds purity tests. Who's truly human? Who's augmented? Who's a traitor to the species? Ugly times ahead.

3-48.title: Algorithmic Divide
3-48.text: Society splits between those who embrace AI and those who reject it. The new culture war: enhancement versus authenticity.

3-49.title: The Empathy Gap
3-49.text: Tech billionaires genuinely can't understand why people fear AI. From their yachts, the future looks bright. From the unemployment line, it looks apocalyptic.

3-50.title: Disruption Theology
3-50.text: 'Move fast and break things' works for software, not societies. Tech's religious faith in disruption ignores the human wreckage.

3-51.title: The Last Vote
3-51.text: Why maintain democracy when most voters contribute nothing to GDP? The economic logic of disenfranchisement becomes terrifyingly clear.
